s: The only street stall on earth with a Michelin star
w: Ski goggles, a roaring charcoal wok, a crab omelette priced like a hotel room. Supinya Junsuta learned to cook after a fire ended her career as a seamstress, then opened a noodle cart and never advertised. The 2018 Michelin Guide gave her a star anyway - the first, and still only, street vendor on earth to hold one.
_facts: Owner Supinya Junsuta, nicknamed Jay Fai, born c. 1945 to Chinese immigrant parents
Worked as a seamstress before a fire led her to cooking in her thirties
Opened her restaurant in the 1980s serving congee and noodle dishes
Received one Michelin star in the inaugural 2018 Bangkok Michelin Guide - the only street food stall in the world to hold one
Signature dish is a crab-meat omelette packed with 300-400g of hand-picked crab, cooked over charcoal

s: The pad thai stall that became the national dish's benchmark
w: Samai Baisamut sold noodles off a canal boat before setting a stall by the old city gate everyone called Pratu Phi - the Ghost Gate. Field Marshal Plaek Phibunsongkhram, who coined the name 'pad thai' itself, is said to have crowned this version the country's best. Sun-dried Chantaburi noodles, prawn oil - the recipe hasn't blinked since.
_facts: Founded as a street stall in the 1950s by Samai Baisamut, who first sold noodles from a boat on a canal in Samut Sakhon
Moved into a permanent shophouse in 1966
Original stall was known as 'Pad Thai Pratu Phi' after its location near the Ghost Gate (Pratu Phi) in old Bangkok
Uses sun-dried sen chan rice noodles from Chantaburi province and a homemade prawn oil
Field Marshal Plaek Phibunsongkhram, who popularized the name 'pad thai', reportedly endorsed it as the country's best

s: CNN's 4th-best fresh market on earth, opposite Chatuchak
w: Set up in 1974 by the Marketing Organization for Farmers so growers could sell top-grade produce straight to the public, middlemen cut out. No puddles, no haggling theatre - meticulously stacked durian and mangosteen, vendors who've spent decades on one dish. CNN Travel named it the world's 4th-best fresh market in 2017.
_facts: Opened in 1974, operated by Thailand's Marketing Organization for Farmers (Or Tor Kor)
Set up to let farmers sell premium produce directly to the public
Voted the 4th-best fresh market in the world by CNN Travel in November 2017
Located opposite Chatuchak Weekend Market, next to Kamphaeng Phet MRT station
Known especially for premium fruit, including durian and mango

s: The wholesale market that feeds half of Bangkok's kitchens
w: Fourteen acres of ice, blood and banana leaves, open before dawn to the small hours - Thailand's largest fresh market, the one supplying the restaurants supplying you. It grew from a small neighbourhood market into the city's central food artery, no tourist polish. CNN called it one of Bangkok's most authentic markets.
_facts: Bangkok's biggest and Thailand's largest fresh wholesale market
Sprawls over roughly 14 acres near Khlong Toei and Queen Sirikit National Convention Centre MRT stations
Open daily from 6:00am to 2:00am
Grew from a small mid-20th-century neighbourhood market into the city's central food distribution point
Named one of Bangkok's most authentic markets by CNN in January 2010

s: The toast-and-fresh-milk cart that grew into a Bangkok icon
w: Khun Mont started out pushing a cart of toasted bread and fresh milk near the Giant Swing in 1964; word of mouth did the rest. The formula never changed - thick toast, coconut custard, cold milk - it just got a proper shopfront on Dinso Road. Sixty years on, Thais still queue for the plate he served off a bicycle.
_facts: Founded in 1964 by Khun Mont, who began as a roving pushcart vendor
First permanent restaurant opened on Dinso Road near the Giant Swing landmark
Known for toasted bread with coconut custard (kaya) filling and fresh milk beverages
Grew into a chain with multiple branches in Bangkok plus one in Chiang Mai

s: The floating market Bangkok keeps for itself, weekends only
w: Set up along Khlong Chak Phra in the 1980s, this floating market never bothered chasing tour buses - weekends only, boats loaded with grilled prawns and som tam pulled against the bank while families eat on stilted platforms over the water. Skip the souvenirs; come for the grill smoke and the fact everyone's speaking Thai.
_facts: Established in the 1980s along Khlong Chak Phra canal
Open Saturday-Sunday, 8:00am-4:30pm, closed weekdays except public holidays
About twelve kilometres from downtown Bangkok
Draws mostly Thai local visitors rather than international tourists
Known for freshly grilled and cooked dishes served from boats and canal-side platforms

s: Chinatown's peppery kway chap, unchanged since the pushcart days
w: Nai Ek arrived from China in 1960 and started selling kway chap - rolled rice noodles in black-peppercorn broth with crispy pork - from a street cart. It became a permanent shop in 1989, and the Michelin Bib Gourmand turned up twice, 2018 and 2019. Order by pointing; you'll be seated inside five minutes, however long the line looks.
_facts: Founded by Nai Ek, who arrived in Bangkok from China in 1960 and began selling kway chap from a push cart
Moved into a permanent shophouse at 442 Soi 9, Yaowarat Road, in 1989
Signature dish is guay jub - rolled rice noodles in black pepper broth with crispy pork
Received the Michelin Bib Gourmand award in 2018 and 2019

s: The 1932 shophouse that set the mango sticky rice standard
w: Kab and Sarapee Chiabchalard opened this Tanao Road shophouse in 1932, and it's still the only mango-sticky-rice specialist to hold a Michelin Bib Gourmand in the city. Chiang Rai sticky rice, Nam Dok Mai mangoes hand-picked outside by a woman who knows exactly which ones to reject, coconut cream kneaded fresh daily.
_facts: Founded in 1932 by Kab and Sarapee Chiabchalard in a shophouse on Tanao Road
The only Bangkok establishment listed in the Michelin Bib Gourmand specifically for mango sticky rice
Uses sticky rice from Chiang Rai and Nam Dok Mai and Ok Rong mango varieties
Coconut cream and milk are hand-kneaded from freshly grated coconut flesh

w: Three Chinese immigrants pooled their savings in 1933 to open a Singapore-style kopi tiam - a format nobody in Thailand had seen - on a stretch of Charoen Krung then the city's cinema district, full of movie houses and stars killing time before a screening. Soft-boiled eggs, dark coffee, kaya toast: the menu hasn't moved in ninety years.
_facts: Founded in 1933 by three overseas Chinese investors as a Singapore-style kopi tiam, a first for Thailand
Located on Charoen Krung Road in Wang Burapha, a cinema and entertainment district in its 1950s-60s heyday
Serves classic kopitiam fare: soft-boiled eggs, dark coffee, kaya toast
In business for over 90 years, now run by the fourth generation of the founding family

s: Cantonese roast duck on Bangkok's oldest paved road since 1909
w: A Guangdong migrant hawked noodles across Bangkok before opening a roast-duck stall on Charoen Krung - the city's oldest paved road - in 1909. His family renamed it Prachak a generation later, when a Thai son-in-law married in and took over. The Cantonese duck recipe hasn't budged since; a fourth generation now runs the till.
_facts: Founded in 1909 by a Cantonese immigrant from Guangdong who had earlier hawked noodles in Bangkok
Located on Charoen Krung Road, Bangkok's oldest paved road, opposite a Robinson's department store
Renamed Prachak in the second generation after a Thai son-in-law married into the family and took over
Original Chinese name was Sin Kee; recipe and preparation unchanged since opening
Now run by the fourth generation of the founding family

s: Decades-old wonton noodles with crab claw, across from Hua Lamphong
w: A family-run bamee joint that's sat virtually opposite Hua Lamphong station for decades, ladling out wonton noodles topped with whole crab claws instead of the usual minced pork. The crab-claw bowl built the reputation and the queue; it's spread to three outlets since, but the original by the station is still the one people mean.
_facts: Also known as Sawang Bami Kam Pu (crab-claw wonton noodles)
Original location sits virtually across the street from Hua Lamphong train station
Family-run business known for topping its wonton noodles with whole crab claws instead of minced pork
Expanded from the original shop to three outlets

s: Seventy years of curry-and-rice, no tables, stools only
w: Tucked down a Chinatown alley for more than seventy years, this khao gaeng stall serves whichever curries came out of the pots that morning - no menu, just point. No tables either, only stools, which locals call the shop's game of musical chairs: arrive at 2pm sharp or eat standing once the good curries run out. They always run out.
_facts: Has served khao gaeng (curry and rice) for more than seventy years in Bangkok's Chinatown
No tables - seating is stools only, earning it the nickname 'Musical Chairs Curry'
Opens from 2pm until the curries sell out

s: The beef broth that has never once been emptied since the 1960s
w: The grandfather started this pot by the Chao Phraya more than sixty years ago; it has simmered, been topped up, and never fully drained since - a perpetual stew of beef, bone and Chinese herbs, three generations deep. Every bowl at the Ekkamai shop is ladled from that same continuous broth, balanced daily by taste alone.
_facts: Founded over 60 years ago by the current owner's grandfather near the Chao Phraya river
Famous for a beef broth kept in continuous 'perpetual stew' simmer, never fully emptied, for decades
Now located on Ekkamai Road, run by the third generation
Broth is balanced daily using a secret blend of Chinese herbs, tasted rather than measured

s: Coconut ice cream, no cow involved, since roughly World War II
w: Tucked into the old-town plaza of Phraeng Phuthon, this shop has churned coconut milk into ice cream for over 80 years - dating to around the Second World War - fresh young coconuts, nothing from a cow. The texture is the tell: faintly crumbly, tiny ice crystals that crunch. Quietly picked up a nod in the Louis Vuitton City Guide.
_facts: Also known as Nuttaporn or Natthaphon Coconut Ice Cream
Located in Phraeng Phuthon in Bangkok's historic old town
In operation for over 80 years, dating back to around World War II
Made from fresh young coconut milk with no dairy
Featured in the Louis Vuitton City Guide
